name: Martin Luther King Jr.
description: American Baptist minister and civil rights leader (1929–1968)
abstract: Martin Luther King Jr. (born Michael King Jr.; January 15, 1929 – April 4, 1968) was an American civil rights activist and Baptist minister who was a prominent leader of the civil rights movement from 1955 until his assassination in 1968.

concerts.label: Testimonial Meeting: Salute to A. Philip Randolph
Tribute to Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.
Frank Sinatra and Lena Horne, Singers
Frank Sinatra and Lena Horne, Singers

about_quotes.speaker: Byron Stripling
about_quotes.evidence: I came from that era of black — there’s that word again — of black families, that stressed a couple of things you and I talked about before. First it was education. They come from that sort of Martin Luther King era where education is your way out
about_quotes.doc_title: Byron Stripling interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 25, 1998 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Tim Ries
about_quotes.evidence: Like even the history that just happened in the 60s with Martin Luther King Jr. Like are you not willing to at least look at the reality of racial discrimination over the past years?
about_quotes.doc_title: Tim Ries interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, March 19, 2022

about_quotes.speaker: Byron Stripling
about_quotes.evidence: during the Montgomery bus boycott one of the things Martin Luther King did during that period of time is he said before I speak... we’re going to sing for like twenty minutes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Byron Stripling interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 25, 2020 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Terri Lyne Carrington
about_quotes.evidence: I mean when you look at like a Martin Luther King for instance, you know and a lot of political activists were ministers or pastors or have strong spiritual beliefs as well.
about_quotes.doc_title: Terri Lyne Carrington interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Rowles
about_quotes.evidence: It wasn't until Martin Luther King came along with his 'Four Hundred Years of Slavery' and 'I Had A Dream' and all that... Well, I'm all for him.

about_quotes.speaker: Wendell Harrison
about_quotes.evidence: You know I’m talking about people like Martin Luther King and all the struggles that went in terms of civil rights or voting rights or women.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wendell Harrison interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, November 10, 2022 [interview 1 of 2]
